steps.title: Convert gas mileage to a consumption rate
steps.content:
If the van gets 32 miles per gallon, then driving 1 mile consumes $\frac{1}{32}$ gallon. Therefore, driving $d$ miles consumes $\frac{d}{32}$ gallons.

The key is recognizing that we need to express miles in terms of gallons used, not the other way around.

steps.title: Write the function for remaining gas
steps.content:
The remaining gas equals the starting amount minus the amount consumed:
$$f(d) = 12 - \frac{d}{32}$$

steps.title: Check all choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — $f(d)=\frac{d}{32}-12$: This subtracts the starting fuel instead of the fuel used, so it gives negative values immediately and grows instead of decreasing ✗
  • Choice B — $f(d)=d-12$: Subtracts gallons directly from miles, so the units are wrong ✗
  • Choice C — $f(d)=12-\frac{d}{32}$: Starts at 12 gallons and subtracts $\frac{d}{32}$ gallons consumed, so it is correct ✓
  • Choice D — $f(d)=12-d$: Subtracts miles directly from gallons, so the units are wrong ✗
That is the correct answer.
